newHtml:
Three cases permitting Muslim blood — apostasy is the third Apostasy & Blasphemy Hudud Prophetic Character Strong Nasa'i #4067
"It is not permissible to shed the blood of a Muslim except in three cases: A man who commits adultery after having married; or one who kills another person; or who reverts to Kufr after having accepted Islam, who is to be killed."

What the hadith says

Caliph Uthman narrates three capital offenses: post-marriage adultery, murder, and apostasy. Religious change is listed alongside taking a life.

Why this is a problem

  1. Apostasy is grouped with murder. Changing religion is moralised at the level of homicide with no treason requirement.
  2. "Reverts to Kufr" requires no additional act. No arms-bearing, no sedition — bare belief-change is the trigger.

Why it fails

The "treason only" reading is a 20th-century overlay. The hadith's wording is bare: "reverts to Kufr — kill him" — with no "and joins the enemy" qualifier. Classical jurists across all four Sunni schools read it literally and codified death for apostasy without any treason requirement. The 13 Muslim-majority jurisdictions that retain apostasy penalties are implementing the canonical text.

Uraniyyin — eyes branded, limbs cut off, left to die of thirst Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Nasa'i #306
"Their eyes were smoldered with heated nails, their hands and feet cut off, then they were left in Al-Harrah in that state until they died."

What the hadith says

A tribal group that apostatised and killed a Muslim herdsman was sentenced by Muhammad: heated nails in the eyes, limbs amputated, left in volcanic terrain without water until dead.

Why this is a problem

  1. Three stacked punishments: blinding, amputation, and engineered death by thirst. Water was denied by Muhammad's order; the victims begged and were refused.
  2. International humanitarian law classifies blinding, mutilation, and denial of water to dying captives as war crimes — regardless of the underlying offense.

Why it fails

Proportionate retaliation does not require engineering death by thirst on top of blinding and amputation. The dying-of-thirst component — victims begged for water and were refused by prophetic order — exceeds any lex talionis calculation. The canonical record attributes this directly to Muhammad, making it prophetic precedent rather than a historical anomaly.

"Whoever changes his religion, kill him" — Nasa'i's version Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Nasa'i #4059
"Whoever changes his religion, execute him."

What the hadith says

Nasa'i preserves the apostasy death penalty across multiple chains — the same blunt command as Bukhari, Abu Dawud, and Ibn Majah.

Why this is a problem

  1. Cross-collection preservation makes the "fringe hadith" dismissal impossible. This is canonical doctrine, not a marginal position.
  2. Contradicts Q 2:256 — and classical jurisprudence resolved the tension in favour of this hadith, not the verse.

Why it fails

The classical consensus across all four Sunni schools treated the hadith literally — death for apostasy with no treason requirement. The "treason-gloss" appears in 20th-century apologetic literature, not in the jurisprudence the hadith generated. Contemporary enforcement in multiple jurisdictions continues to apply the rule to private belief change.

Kill active and passive partner — Nasa'i's capital sentence Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud #4462
"Whoever you find doing the act of the people of Lut — kill the doer and the one done to."

What the hadith says

Death penalty for same-sex acts preserved across Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Nasa'i parallels — both parties executed regardless of consent.

Why this is a problem

  1. Five canonical collections carry this directive. The "fringe hadith" classification is impossible — this is settled classical doctrine.
  2. "The one done to" includes coerced partners. Rape victims face execution alongside perpetrators.

Why it fails

High evidentiary standards have not prevented application in states where government surveillance substitutes for witnesses. A legal tradition whose canonical text says "kill both" — regardless of consent or coercion — has installed a death sentence for same-sex intimacy as settled doctrine. The five-collection attestation removes any basis for calling this marginal or theoretical.

Sex with captive women permitted — Nasa'i's confirmation Women Prophetic Character Strong Nasa'i #3331
"We captured some women and wanted to practice coitus interruptus [to preserve resale value]."

What the hadith says

Muslim fighters consult Muhammad about withdrawal during sex with captives. His answer addresses pregnancy theology — not consent. The captive-sex transaction is the unquestioned baseline.

Why this is a problem

  1. Sex with captives is default-legitimate — only the method is at issue. Consent is never raised by Muhammad or the questioners.
  2. The soldiers' concern is economic (resale value), not moral. Muhammad's response operates within that frame without objection.

Why it fails

The "gradual trajectory" is a 20th-century reading that fourteen centuries of classical jurisprudence did not deliver. The hadith's premise — that sex with captives is permitted — was accepted without objection by Muhammad. A religion that regulates the timing of sexual contact with captives has accepted the underlying transaction and moved on to adjust its technical parameters.

Captive women sold — soldiers had sex before the market Warfare & Jihad Slavery & Captives Strong Nasa'i #3362
"We took captive women from among the Arabs. We used to have intercourse with them, but we did not want them to get pregnant, so we said: Shall we practice coitus interruptus? Then we asked the Messenger of Allah about that, and he said: 'You do not have to do that; there is no soul but Allah has decreed that it will come into existence.'"

What the hadith says

The Prophet's ruling treats sex with captives as baseline behaviour — the only question is whether to withdraw before ejaculation.

Why this is a problem

  1. The rape is assumed; only the contraceptive practice is discussed. No objection to the act is raised by Muhammad.
  2. The soldiers' motive — preserving resale value — is preserved without moral comment.

Why it fails

Regulating a practice is not abolishing it. A theology whose fatwa on "sex with captured women" addresses method instead of morality has already conceded the moral argument. ISIS's explicit classical-law citations when enslaving Yazidi women in 2014 demonstrate the canon's operational relevance fourteen centuries later.

Angels curse the wife who refuses her husband's bed Women Moral Problems Strong Nasa'i (parallel to Bukhari/Muslim)
"If a man calls his wife to his bed and she refuses, the angels curse her until morning."

What the hadith says

A wife who refuses her husband's sexual request is subject to angelic cursing for the rest of the night.

Why this is a problem

  1. Consent is effectively removed from marital sex. The husband's subjective displeasure — not objective harm — triggers a divine sanction against refusal.
  2. Multi-collection attestation (Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Majah, Nasa'i) makes this canonical settled doctrine.

Why it fails

The "legitimate reasons" exception is absent from the hadith's plain text; it is a juristic addition to manage implications. The plain trigger is the husband's anger. The classical doctrine of tamkeen — sexual access as the husband's enforceable right — derived directly from this hadith, effectively removing the wife's consent from the legal framework of marriage.

Muhammad bewitched for months — false memories of things undone Magic & Occult Prophetic Character Contradictions Strong Nasa'i #4080
"Magic was worked on the Messenger of Allah until he used to imagine that he had done something when he had not done it."

What the hadith says

Muhammad's memory was magically compromised for months by a Jewish sorcerer using a hair and comb buried in a well, causing false memories of actions he had not performed.

Why this is a problem

  1. A prophet's memory altered by a sorcerer undermines certainty about revelations received during that period.
  2. Contradicts Q 5:67 — Allah's promise to protect Muhammad — yet sorcerous interference operated unchecked for months.

Why it fails

The apologetic requires a clean separation between "mundane cognitive function" and "prophetic reception" that the hadith does not supply. If a sorcerer could falsify Muhammad's memories for months, the verification that no revelation was tainted is a stipulation, not a demonstration. Q 5:67's protection promise covers people generally — yet the canonical text records a multi-month period of uncontested sorcerous interference.

Allah will uncover His Shin on the Day of Judgment Allah's Character Cosmology Strong Nasa'i (parallel to Bukhari/Muslim)
"Our Lord will uncover His Shin; every believer will prostrate; but those who prostrated in this world for show will be unable to do so, their backs becoming like a plate of iron."

What the hadith says

A specific physical feature of Allah is unveiled on Judgment Day, triggering prostration by sincere believers while hypocrites' backs lock rigid.

Why this is a problem

  1. A specific revealable body part attributed to Allah contradicts Q 42:11 ("nothing is like Him") unless incomparability is rendered essentially empty.
  2. Multi-collection attestation (Bukhari, Muslim, Nasa'i) with identical wording makes a purely figurative reading difficult — the early community transmitted the anatomical term consistently.

Why it fails

The bila kayf position avoids the contradiction by making the term privately meaningless — affirm it but deny any content. The metaphor position is coherent but requires overriding the plain language of a multiply-attested canonical hadith. A God whose Shin triggers prostrations is a God whose body the tradition could not stop itself from describing, whatever later theological frameworks did with the result.

Female testimony worth half of male — Nasa'i's codification Women Governance Strong Q 2:282
"If one of them forgets, the other can remind her." (Q 2:282 applied via Nasa'i's testimony chapters)

What the hadith says

Two women's testimony equals one man's — codified by Quranic instruction and prophetic interpretation, embedding structural legal inequality in both scripture and hadith.

Why this is a problem

  1. Legal worth assigned by sex, not by evidence quality or witness character. A woman's testimony is half a man's regardless of expertise or credibility.
  2. Active in Iran and Saudi Arabia — family law, financial disputes, and criminal proceedings in multiple modern jurisdictions.

Why it fails

Classical jurisprudence extended the half-testimony rule to criminal evidence and family law, not limiting it to commercial transactions. The "limited context" reading is a modern apologetic restriction the tradition never applied. Active legal systems enforcing the half-testimony rule are implementing the classical jurisprudence correctly — reform requires arguing against the canon, not from within it.

"Whoever does the act of Lot's people, kill both parties" LGBTQ / Gender Hudud Strong Nasa'i (parallel to Abu Dawud #4462)
"Whoever you find doing the act of Lot's people, kill the doer and the one to whom it is done."

What the hadith says

Nasa'i preserves the same death sentence for homosexuality as Tirmidhi, Bukhari, Abu Dawud, and Ibn Majah — five canonical collections all carrying this directive.

Why this is a problem

  1. Five canonical collections. The "fringe hadith" dismissal is categorically impossible — this is settled classical doctrine.
  2. "Kill both" includes rape victims. The passive partner faces death regardless of consent or coercion.

Why it fails

Evidentiary barriers have not prevented enforcement in states with surveillance infrastructure. The Quran's silence created interpretive space that the classical tradition filled with this multiply-attested hadith. A tradition that has carried this rule in five canonical collections for fourteen centuries cannot describe it as marginal when active judicial systems apply it to real people.

One-fifth of war booty goes to the Prophet personally Warfare & Jihad Governance Strong Nasa'i #4139
"The spoils of war are divided into five parts: four-fifths for the fighters, one-fifth for Allah and the Messenger."

What the hadith says

The Prophet's personal income was a fixed 20% share of every raid — including human captives. Q 8:41 codifies this in the Quran.

Why this is a problem

  1. Religious leader's income tied directly to war plunder. The structural incentive favours continued military expansion.
  2. Captive women in the 20% share were available for sexual access. The khums included human beings, not only money and goods.

Why it fails

Whether funds were spent charitably does not dissolve the structural issue: a religious leader's income was directly proportional to the volume of war-plunder his forces generated. The same fifth included captured human beings. A system that fuses prophecy with procurement has a design problem that charitable-use framing cannot repair.
